period: begun c. 1503; worked on until c. 1519
title: The Mona Lisa
summary: A small oil portrait painted by Leonardo da Vinci, begun around 1503 in Florence and worked on for years. It shows a woman, most likely Lisa del Giocondo, the wife of a silk merchant, seated before a hazy landscape with a faint, ambiguous smile. The painting is a masterpiece of soft light and living expression, built with Leonardo's sfumato technique of smoke-like gradations with no hard lines. It was always admired, but its modern status as the most famous painting on earth was hugely amplified by an accident of history. Its theft from the Louvre in 1911 made it a global media sensation, and endless mass reproduction carried it the rest of the way. It now hangs behind glass in the Louvre, the most visited and reproduced artwork in history.
hook: On August 21, 1911, a workman walked out of the Louvre with the most famous painting in the world hidden under his smock. Except it was not yet the most famous painting in the world. It was a well-regarded Renaissance portrait by Leonardo da Vinci, admired by experts but not mobbed by crowds. The theft changed that. For two years the empty wall drew more visitors than the painting ever had, the world's newspapers ran the hunt on their front pages, and when the Mona Lisa came home she was a celebrity. The crime did not make her a great painting. She already was one. It made her the most recognized face on earth.

beginner.theWork:
A woman sits with her hands folded, turned slightly toward you. Behind her stretches a strange, dreamlike landscape of winding paths, distant water, and hazy mountains. She looks straight out at you, and she is almost smiling. The corners of her mouth are soft and blurred, so the smile seems to flicker. Look at it one way and she is amused. Look again and you are not sure.

This is the Mona Lisa. It was painted by Leonardo da Vinci, one of the greatest artists who ever lived. He began it around the year 1503, in the Italian city of Florence, and kept working on it for years. The painting is small. It is about the size of a large window pane, not much bigger than a poster. People who travel across the world to see it are often surprised by how little it is.

Nobody is completely certain who the woman was. The most likely answer is that she was Lisa del Giocondo, the wife of a silk merchant in Florence. That is why the painting is also called La Gioconda in Italian and La Joconde in French. But this is the best guess of scholars, not a proven fact.
beginner.momentOfMaking:
Leonardo painted faces in a special way. He built the picture from many very thin layers of oil paint. Each layer was almost see-through. As they stacked up, the hard edges melted away. Skin seemed to blend softly into shadow and into air. This smoky, blurred effect has a name. It is called sfumato, from an Italian word meaning smoke.

That technique is the secret of her smile. Around her mouth and eyes, Leonardo left the lines soft and unclear. Your eye cannot lock onto a fixed expression. So the face seems to shift and breathe as you look. She feels alive in a way that earlier portraits did not.

Leonardo carried the painting with him for the rest of his life. He never seems to have handed it over to whoever first asked for it. He took it to France in his old age. After he died there in 1519, it entered the French royal collection. Centuries later it ended up in the Louvre, the great museum in Paris, where it still hangs today.
beginner.whatItDoes:
The Mona Lisa does something quiet but hard. It makes paint feel like a living person. Most portraits before it showed people stiffly, in profile, like a face on a coin. Leonardo turned his sitter toward us. He gave her a real gaze that meets ours. He surrounded her with soft light. The result is not a record of a face. It is the feeling of being looked at by someone who is thinking.

The smile is the heart of it. Because the edges are blurred, no single expression stays put. People have argued for centuries about what she feels. Happy? Sad? Knowing? Mocking? The honest answer is that the painting was built so you can never quite decide. The mystery is not a riddle with a hidden solution. It is the point. Leonardo found a way to paint a person who seems to have an inner life we cannot fully read, just like a real human being.

general.theWork:
She turns toward you out of a hazy, half-real landscape, hands quietly folded, and meets your eyes with a smile that will not hold still. The Mona Lisa is a small oil panel, roughly thirty inches tall, painted by Leonardo da Vinci and begun around 1503 in Florence. Leonardo worked on it on and off for years and never let it go, carrying it with him until his death in France in 1519. The sitter is most likely Lisa del Giocondo, wife of the Florentine silk merchant Francesco del Giocondo, which gives the painting its other names, La Gioconda and La Joconde. That identification rests on a brief note by the artist Giorgio Vasari and is probable rather than certain.

What makes the panel extraordinary is the handling of light. Leonardo built the face from dozens of near-transparent glazes, a method called sfumato, in which forms dissolve into soft gradation with no hard outline anywhere. The corners of the mouth and the edges of the eyes are left deliberately blurred, so the expression seems to change with each glance. The same haze governs the landscape behind her, where winding roads and distant peaks fade into a bluish mist. Figure and atmosphere are made of the same soft substance, and the woman seems to breathe inside it.
general.momentOfMaking:
The portrait was an unusual object for its moment. Most fifteenth-century Italian portraits showed the sitter in strict profile, like a medal, with a flat background. Leonardo turned his subject to a three-quarter view, gave her a direct gaze, and set her before an imagined landscape rather than a plain ground. He treated a private commission, a merchant's wife, with the care and ambition usually reserved for grander subjects. The result helped reset what a portrait could attempt: not a likeness fixed for the record, but a living presence caught in a moment of thought.

Leonardo's refusal to part with the painting is itself telling. A portrait was normally delivered to the family that paid for it. Instead he kept reworking the Mona Lisa for the rest of his life, which is part of why scholars debate exactly when it was finished, or whether he ever considered it finished at all. When he moved to France late in life at the invitation of King Francis I, the panel went with him. It passed into the French royal collection and, after the Revolution, into the Louvre. The artist Leonardo and the wider rebirth of naturalistic art he embodied belong to their own entries; this one follows the panel.
general.whatItDoes:
The painting's power rests on two things that are hard to separate, life and mystery. The life comes from technique. By dissolving every edge into gradation, Leonardo removed the diagram-like stiffness of earlier portraits and produced a face that reads as soft flesh in real light. The three-quarter turn and the steady, outward gaze complete the effect. She is not displayed for inspection. She appears to be regarding the viewer, which reverses the usual relationship and makes the encounter feel mutual.

The mystery comes from the same brush. Because Leonardo blurred the very features that carry expression, the mouth and the eyes, the smile resists being pinned down. Studies of human vision suggest the effect is partly built into how we see. Look directly at her mouth and the smile seems to fade. Look at her eyes and it returns at the edge of vision. Whether or not Leonardo understood this mechanism, he engineered an expression that genuinely shifts. The painting distilled a Renaissance ambition, to capture not just a face but the living interior behind it. It did so well enough that viewers five centuries later still cannot agree on what she feels.

advanced.theWork: The central interpretive difficulty with the Mona Lisa is that the object and its fame have become almost impossible to disentangle. Serious scholarship now treats that entanglement as part of the subject rather than as noise to be cleared away. Donald Sassoon's cultural history of the painting argues that its modern status is not the natural overflow of aesthetic quality but a contingent accumulation. A strong but not uniquely celebrated Renaissance portrait was carried to global pre-eminence by specific nineteenth- and twentieth-century events. Against this, an older art-historical tradition insists that the panel's formal achievement is what made it available for such mythologizing in the first place. Nothing of comparable quality, on that view, lay nearby to be elevated instead. The two positions are not fully opposed. The defensible reading holds both claims at once. The painting is genuinely a masterpiece of optical naturalism, and its singular celebrity is an accident of reception, and the reading resists collapsing either into the other.
advanced.momentOfMaking: The dating and the identification of the sitter remain unsettled in instructive ways. The standard identification with Lisa del Giocondo derives chiefly from Vasari, writing decades after the fact and not always reliable. It was strengthened in 2005 by a marginal note in a Heidelberg copy of Cicero. That note records that Leonardo was painting the head of Lisa del Giocondo in 1503. The discovery hardened a probability into a near-consensus, but it confirms a beginning, not the finished panel. Leonardo's habit of prolonged revision means the work in the Louvre may incorporate years of later labor. The deeper analytical point is that the painting cannot be assigned a single moment of making. It is a palimpsest of campaigns. Technical study using infrared and X-ray imaging keeps recovering earlier states beneath the surface, and the question of when the Mona Lisa was made resists the tidy answer that its fame seems to demand.
advanced.whatItDoes: The smile has attracted more pseudo-explanation than perhaps any other passage in painting, and the task is to separate the demonstrable from the romantic. The demonstrable account is optical. Leonardo concentrated his softest sfumato precisely on the features that encode expression. Human foveal vision resolves fine detail only at the point of fixation, so the perceived expression changes depending on where the eye lands. The result is a smile that appears to flicker, and the effect is real, replicable, and studied. The romantic account descends from Walter Pater's 1869 evocation of a figure older than the rocks among which she sits. It reads the ambiguity as metaphysical, the face as a screen for fantasies about femininity, secrecy, and the unconscious. The interpretive discipline the painting demands is to grant the optical mechanism its due without pretending it exhausts the matter. It also asks us to recognize Pater's reverie as a document in the painting's reception rather than evidence about Leonardo's intent.
advanced.whatItChanged: What the Mona Lisa changed most decisively was not the practice of portraiture but the modern economy of the artistic image, and the pivot is the 1911 theft. The panel was stolen by the Louvre handyman Vincenzo Peruggia and was missing for over two years. The disappearance generated a volume of press coverage no painting had previously commanded, and the recovery in 1913 returned a national celebrity rather than a connoisseur's prize. Marcel Duchamp's 1919 L.H.O.O.Q. then enlisted the image as the readymade target of avant-garde irony. That work, a reproduction defaced with a moustache and an obscene caption, confirmed that the painting had become a universal sign available for parody. The reproduction that followed, on advertisements, packaging, and screens, completed a transformation Walter Benjamin's account of mechanical reproduction anticipates almost exactly. The endless copying that universalized the image is precisely what now stands between a viewer and the panel as a painting. The crucial claim is that this celebrity is manufactured and largely accidental, and that conceding the accident does not diminish the underlying achievement.
advanced.howBeenRead: The painting has been read, across its modern life, less as a portrait than as a mirror for whoever stands before it. The most rigorous response is to historicize each reading rather than adjudicate among them. Daniel Arasse insisted on returning to the specific facts of the panel, its optical construction and its place in Florentine practice. He treated that return as a corrective to readings that float free of the object. Yet even the resistance to mythology is now part of the painting's story. The Romantic reading made her an enigma of the eternal feminine; the avant-garde made her a target; mass culture made her a logo; the museum made her a security problem behind glass and barriers. The genuinely uncomfortable conclusion is that there may be no recoverable Mona Lisa beneath the accumulated fame. The panel a visitor glimpses over a crowd is constituted as much by a century of reproduction and theft as by Leonardo's glazes. The task is not to strip the fame away but to understand the object as the sum of the painting and everything that has been done to it.

foundation.theStory: The Mona Lisa is a problem in how a genuinely great painting became something larger and stranger than a painting, the single most recognized image on earth. Around 1503, in Florence, Leonardo da Vinci began a small portrait of a woman, most likely Lisa del Giocondo, and worked on it for years. Understanding the picture means holding two things at once. The first is the achievement, a face built from smoke-soft layers of paint that seems to live and to smile in a way no single glance can fix. The second is the fame, which is partly an accident of history. The painting was always admired, but its leap to global superstardom owed an enormous debt to its theft from the Louvre in 1911 and to a century of mass reproduction.
foundation.theDebate: Two debates organize how people think about the Mona Lisa. The first is about the sitter and the making: who exactly she was, and when the painting was finished. The identification with Lisa del Giocondo is probable, supported by an early note from Vasari and a document found in 2005, but Leonardo's long reworking keeps the date open. The second debate is about the fame itself. Did the painting become the most famous in the world because it is the best? Or because of contingent events, above all the 1911 theft, that happened to fix the world's attention on this panel rather than another? Scholars increasingly treat the fame as something to be explained, not assumed.
foundation.whyItMatters: The Mona Lisa unlocks several larger subjects. Among them are Leonardo and the Renaissance ideal of capturing a living human interior, the technique of sfumato and how oil paint changed what portraits could do, and the modern machinery of celebrity. It is the clearest case study in how an artwork can acquire a fame out of all proportion to anything visible on its surface, through theft, reproduction, parody, and tourism. It also poses a question that reaches well beyond art. How much of what we revere is the thing itself, and how much is the accumulated weight of attention that history happens to have piled onto it?
questions: Leonardo blurred the corners of the mouth and eyes so the smile seems to shift as you look. Why might an expression you cannot quite read feel more alive than a clearly happy or sad one?

The painting became a global sensation after it was stolen in 1911. What does it mean for the most famous artwork in the world to owe so much of its fame to a crime rather than to what is on the canvas?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Modern study of the Mona Lisa runs along two tracks. On the painting itself, the work of Martin Kemp and Frank Zöllner anchors the technical and biographical account, situating the panel within Leonardo's practice of sfumato and his lifelong revision. Daniel Arasse, for his part, argued for reading the picture through its own optical construction rather than through later myth. On the fame, Donald Sassoon's cultural history reconstructs how a respected Renaissance portrait became a global icon, treating the 1911 theft, mass reproduction, and avant-garde appropriation as the real engines of its celebrity. Walter Pater's nineteenth-century reverie is now read less as criticism than as a primary document in that story, evidence of how the Romantic imagination remade the painting into an enigma.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence is unusually rich on some questions and stubbornly thin on others. Technical imaging, infrared and X-ray, reveals layers and earlier states beneath the surface and confirms the painstaking glaze method. The optical account of the flickering smile is testable and has been studied experimentally. The sitter's identity rests on firmer ground since the 2005 discovery of a dated marginal note, though it confirms a beginning rather than the finished panel. Three things the evidence cannot fully settle. The first two are when Leonardo considered the work done and what he intended the expression to mean. The third is the counterfactual at the heart of the fame debate: whether another painting would have reached comparable celebrity had the 1911 theft fallen elsewhere.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong account of the Mona Lisa has to refuse the two easy stories. One says the painting is famous purely because it is the greatest, which ignores how much of its modern celebrity was manufactured by theft, reproduction, and parody. The other says the fame is wholly accidental, a fluke of 1911 that could have landed on any competent portrait. That story ignores that the panel is in fact an exceptional solution to the problem of painting a living presence. The defensible position holds both: the painting is genuinely a masterpiece, and its singular global fame is an accident of reception that a different history might have conferred elsewhere. The discipline is to keep the two claims in view at once, because collapsing either into the other yields a tidy story that the evidence will not support.
questions: The 2005 Heidelberg note records Leonardo painting Lisa del Giocondo's head in 1503, yet he reworked the panel for years afterward. How should a historian weigh a document that fixes a beginning when the question is what the finished painting is?
If the painting's fame is partly an accident of the 1911 theft and of mass reproduction, can a visitor today still encounter it as a painting, or only as the icon those events created?

research.topic: The attribution, technique, and provenance of the painting
research.content: It is well established that the Mona Lisa is an oil-on-poplar portrait by Leonardo da Vinci, begun around 1503 in Florence and worked on over a long period. Leonardo brought it to France, where he died in 1519, and it then entered the French royal collection. The painting later passed to the Louvre, where it now hangs. Its construction through many thin, near-transparent glazes is confirmed by close examination and by infrared and X-ray imaging. This is the sfumato technique that dissolves outlines into soft gradation, and the same imaging reveals revisions beneath the surface. The panel is small, roughly seventy-seven by fifty-three centimeters.

research.topic: The optical mechanism of the flickering smile
research.content: Research in vision science has proposed that the elusiveness of the Mona Lisa's smile is partly an artifact of human perception. The eye resolves fine detail sharply only at the point of fixation, and more coarsely in peripheral vision. Because Leonardo concentrated his softest sfumato on the mouth and eyes, the perceived expression appears to change depending on where the viewer looks. Experimental studies, including work associated with the vision scientist Margaret Livingstone, support this account. It remains an explanation of why the smile seems to shift, not a claim about Leonardo's conscious intent, which cannot be recovered.

research.topic: Leonardo's intended meaning for the expression and the precise completion date
research.content: What evidence would resolve it: a statement by Leonardo describing what he intended the expression to convey and when he regarded the painting as finished. Why it is unavailable: Leonardo left no such account, and his lifelong reworking of the panel left no clear point of completion. The early identification by Vasari was written decades later, and the 2005 Heidelberg note records only a beginning in 1503. What can be responsibly inferred: the sitter is most likely Lisa del Giocondo, the ambiguity of the expression was deliberate rather than accidental, and the painting absorbed years of revision. What must remain unknowable: what Leonardo meant the smile to say, and the exact date he considered the work done, if he ever did.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Solution to Painting a Living Face
comparativeNarrative.content: From inside the study of Renaissance painting, the Mona Lisa is first of all a technical breakthrough. Earlier Italian portraits showed sitters stiffly in profile against flat grounds, like faces on coins. Leonardo turned his subject toward the viewer and gave her a direct gaze. He built her from dozens of near-transparent layers, so that skin, shadow, and air blend without a hard line anywhere. This view reveals what the fame can obscure. The painting solved a real problem: how to make paint read as a living presence with an inner life. On this account the panel earned its place among the great works long before it became a celebrity. The achievement is visible in the brushwork, not in the crowds.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Manufactured Icon
comparativeNarrative.content: From the vantage of how images acquire fame, the Mona Lisa is the clearest case of celebrity that outran the object. The painting was respected for centuries but not uniquely worshipped. Then in 1911 it was stolen from the Louvre. For two years the empty wall and the international hunt filled the world's newspapers. Reproduction, advertising, and Duchamp's mustachioed parody finished the work. They turned a portrait into a logo recognized everywhere. This perspective reveals what art history alone cannot. Fame is made, not merely deserved, and a contingent crime did more to crown this painting than any quality on its surface. The greatness is real, but the superstardom was assembled by history.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: The museum and the modern visitor
comparativeNarrative.name: The Painting Behind the Glass
comparativeNarrative.content: For the millions who file past it each year, the Mona Lisa is an experience shaped by its own renown. Visitors reach a small panel set behind bulletproof glass and a barrier, photographed over a sea of raised phones, often for only a few seconds. Many are surprised by how small it is and unsure what they are meant to feel. This perspective brings out a tension the other two do not. The fame that draws the crowd is also what stands between the crowd and the painting. The icon and the artwork now occupy the same frame, and the visitor must decide whether it is possible to see Leonardo's glazes through the century of attention piled on top of them.

comparativeSummary: The same small panel splits along a single question. Where does its standing come from, the canvas or its history? Art historians locate it in the brushwork, a genuine solution to the problem of painting a living face, reached long before the crowds arrived. Cultural historians locate it elsewhere. They point to the 1911 theft, the reproductions, and the parodies that made a global icon out of a respected portrait. The modern visitor lives inside the result, meeting a masterpiece and a celebrity in the same frame, behind glass, unsure which one is on the wall. Held together, the three show that the Mona Lisa is genuinely great and genuinely overexposed, and that neither fact cancels the other.

rabbitHole.label: Leonardo da Vinci
rabbitHole.reason: Leonardo painted the Mona Lisa and carried it with him until his death. His life, his sfumato technique, and his conviction that painting was a form of inquiry are the necessary context this entry keeps lean in order to follow the panel itself.

rabbitHole.label: The Renaissance
rabbitHole.reason: The painting embodies the Renaissance ambition to capture not just a likeness but the living interior of a human being. Understanding the rebirth of naturalistic art and its culture of patronage explains what the Mona Lisa was attempting and why it mattered.

rabbitHole.label: Florence
rabbitHole.reason: Leonardo began the portrait in Florence. The city's merchant wealth and artistic competition produced the world in which a silk merchant could commission a likeness of his wife from the leading painter of the age.

rabbitHole.label: Michelangelo
rabbitHole.reason: Michelangelo was Leonardo's great contemporary and rival, the other supreme artist of the High Renaissance. Setting the two side by side sharpens what was distinctive about Leonardo's soft, atmospheric approach to the human figure.

rabbitHole.label: The Italian City-States
rabbitHole.reason: The competitive, commercial world of Renaissance Italy, organized into rival city-states, created the patronage and the prestige economy that made works like the Mona Lisa possible. The painting is a product of that distinctive political and economic order.

rabbitHole.label: The Museum
rabbitHole.reason: The Mona Lisa's modern life is inseparable from the Louvre. The rise of the public museum turned a royal possession into a mass attraction. The painting behind glass is a case study in how the museum shapes what we see and how we see it.

rabbitHole.label: Modern Art
rabbitHole.reason: Marcel Duchamp's defaced reproduction of the Mona Lisa was a defining gesture of the twentieth-century avant-garde. The painting's reuse as a target for parody connects it to the modern art that defined itself partly against the old masters.

rabbitHole.label: Photography
rabbitHole.reason: The Mona Lisa's global fame was built on mechanical reproduction, and photography is the technology that made the endless copying possible. The painting is the standard example of how reproduction can universalize an image and change its meaning.

reference.author: Sassoon, Donald
reference.title: Becoming Mona Lisa: The Making of a Global Icon
reference.annotation: Reconstructs in full how the painting became the most famous in the world, tracing the contingent events, above all the 1911 theft and mass reproduction, that manufactured its celebrity. Essential for separating the painting's achievement from its accidental fame.

reference.author: Kemp, Martin
reference.title: Leonardo da Vinci: The Marvellous Works of Nature and Man
reference.annotation: The leading scholarly account of Leonardo's practice, integrating his science and his painting. Essential for situating the Mona Lisa within sfumato technique and the artist's lifelong revision rather than within the later myth.

reference.author: Zöllner, Frank
reference.title: Leonardo da Vinci: The Complete Paintings and Drawings
reference.annotation: The standard catalogue of Leonardo's paintings, with detailed technical and documentary analysis of the Mona Lisa, including its dating and the question of the sitter's identity. The foundational reference for the panel's physical facts.

reference.author: Arasse, Daniel
reference.title: Leonardo da Vinci
reference.annotation: A close reading of Leonardo's paintings that insists on returning to the optical construction of the Mona Lisa itself as a corrective to readings that float free of the panel. Useful for the discipline of seeing the painting past its fame.

reference.author: Pater, Walter
reference.title: The Renaissance: Studies in Art and Poetry
reference.annotation: Contains the famous evocation of the Mona Lisa as a figure older than the rocks among which she sits. Now read less as criticism than as a primary document of how the Romantic imagination remade the painting into an enigma.

reference.author: Livingstone, Margaret
reference.title: Vision and Art: The Biology of Seeing
reference.annotation: Presents the perceptual account of the elusive smile, arguing that its flicker arises from how the eye resolves detail at and away from the point of fixation. Useful for the demonstrable optical mechanism behind the painting's most discussed feature.

commerce.title: Becoming Mona Lisa: The Making of a Global Icon
commerce.author: Donald Sassoon
commerce.note: Reach for this first if you want to understand why this painting, of all paintings, became the most famous in the world. Sassoon tells the story of the fame, including the 1911 theft, in clear and gripping prose.

commerce.title: Leonardo da Vinci
commerce.author: Walter Isaacson
commerce.note: For a reader who wants the man behind the painting first. Isaacson's biography is accessible and vivid, and it places the Mona Lisa inside Leonardo's restless career of art and investigation.

commerce.title: Leonardo da Vinci: The Marvellous Works of Nature and Man
commerce.author: Martin Kemp
commerce.note: Read this once the basics are familiar and you want the serious scholarly view. Kemp connects the sfumato technique of the Mona Lisa to Leonardo's whole project of understanding how nature works.

commerce.title: The Lost Painting and other essays on Leonardo
commerce.author: Daniel Arasse
commerce.note: For the reader who wants to learn to look. Arasse models close attention to the panel's optical construction, a useful corrective for anyone tired of myth and ready to see the painting itself.

commerce.title: Leonardo da Vinci: The Complete Paintings and Drawings
commerce.author: Frank Zöllner
commerce.note: Turn here for the full catalogue and the technical evidence. Zöllner is demanding but is the reference to consult on the dating, the materials, and the documentary case for the sitter's identity.

popularCulture.title: The 1911 theft from the Louvre
popularCulture.description: In 1911 a workman named Vincenzo Peruggia stole the Mona Lisa from the Louvre. It was gone for two years. Crowds came just to see the empty space on the wall, and newspapers across the world ran the hunt on their front pages. By the time it came home, the painting was a star. The crime, more than the brushwork, made it the most famous picture on earth.

popularCulture.title: The mystery of the smile
popularCulture.description: People love to ask what the Mona Lisa is thinking. Is she happy, sad, or laughing at us? The honest answer is that Leonardo blurred her mouth and eyes on purpose, so no single look settles it. Films, songs, and ads play up the riddle as if there were a hidden answer. There is not. The shifting smile is the achievement, not a code to crack.

popularCulture.title: Duchamp's L.H.O.O.Q.
popularCulture.description: In 1919 the artist Marcel Duchamp took a cheap postcard of the Mona Lisa. He drew a moustache and a goatee on her, and added a rude caption. It was a joke aimed at the worship of old art. The prank only worked because the image was already everywhere. It also pushed the painting further into fame, turning a great portrait into something anyone could mock.

popularCulture.title: The Mona Lisa as a logo
popularCulture.description: Her face sells everything now: chocolate, perfume, ads, cartoons, phone cases. The image is so copied that it works like a brand more than a painting. This reach spread her fame to every corner of the world. It also flattens her. When a picture is on a coffee mug, it gets harder to stand in front of the real panel and see the soft light and living gaze that made it great.

popularCulture.title: The crowd at the Louvre
popularCulture.description: Visitors to the Louvre often expect a huge painting and find a small one behind thick glass, ringed by a barrier and a wall of raised phones. Many get only a few seconds before the crowd pushes them on. The scene captures something true about modern fame. The very renown that brings the crowd is also what stands between the crowd and a quiet look at the picture.
